APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper directly addresses core NLP research concerns—evaluation methodology for creative language generation—by proposing a comprehensive manual annotation protocol and a fully automated rhyme-density-based metric, which is highly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML venues. It also contributes a new annotated dataset of lyrics for 13 rap artists and empirical comparisons (LSTM vs n-gram baselines) using standard evaluation measures, matching the methodological and audience expectations of these conferences. While focused on rap ghostwriting, the evaluation advances (penalizing repetition, automated rhyme/similarity measures) are broadly applicable to style-preserving generation tasks, positioning the work well within the venue's community.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are generally supported: the paper presents a combined manual and automated evaluation methodology, demonstrates an automated rhyme-density-based metric with repetition penalty, and empirically compares per-artist LSTM models to n-gram baselines. Experimental methodology appears reasonable (line/page-level annotations, rhyme density, max-similarity over iterations, n-gram baselines), but reported limitations—heuristic preprocessing that may leave non-verse text, inability of the automated metric to capture vocabulary/themes, and LSTM difficulties integrating new vocabulary—are sources of minor concern. No mathematical proofs are required and none are indicated; however, the potential dataset noise and remaining unassessed stylistic dimensions slightly weaken the conclusiveness of some claims.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and states the main contributions early (manual evaluation methodology, automated rhyme-density-based metric, and released dataset). Notation and metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, Match%/MatchA%) are defined consistently, and the experimental presentation (tables/metrics/examples) seems informative. Writing quality is described as adequate, though there are minor presentation issues around preprocessing heuristics and limitations that could be clarified for less ambiguity.

IMPACT.justification: The paper targets a concrete, meaningful problem in creative language generation by proposing a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol for rap lyric ghostwriting and an automated rhyme-density-based improvement to penalize repetition, which addresses a persistent evaluation gap. The release of a 13-artist annotated dataset and the empirical comparison showing LSTM generalizes better than n-gram baselines make the work practically useful and likely to be adopted by researchers working on stylistic text generation. However, limitations (heuristic preprocessing, inability of automated measures to capture vocabulary/tone/themes, and LSTM vocabulary integration issues) and its narrow domain focus constrain its broader transformative potential. Overall, the contribution is a solid, reusable methodological and data resource that opens modest new directions rather than changing the field.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its LSTM-based ghostwriter to an n-gram baseline across the same OHHLA-derived dataset using both manual and automated metrics, which is an appropriate and fair experimental setup. However, it does not appear to directly compare against or reimplement the closest prior LSTM-based rap generation work (GhostWriter 2015), which is a notable omission even though related work coverage is described as adequate. The authors analyze differences between their LSTM and n-gram baselines (showing better generalization) and acknowledge limitations of automated metrics, so overall the comparisons are good but missing at least one directly relevant prior-method baseline.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents a thorough manual and automated evaluation methodology, a new 13-artist dataset, and empirical comparisons of LSTM vs n-gram baselines (n=1..9), which supports the main claims and includes sensible sensitivity analysis across n-gram orders. It also improves automation of rhyme-density-based evaluation and explicitly discusses limitations (automated eval blind to vocabulary/tone, heuristic preprocessing, LSTM vocabulary issues). Reproducibility is partly supported by reported hyperparameters but weakened because code release is not mentioned and preprocessing was heuristic, and the experimental scope could be broadened by comparing to other contemporary models (e.g., prior LSTM work like GhostWriter 2015) or including more ablations. Overall the experiments are solid with minor gaps in breadth and reproducibility.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper does not invent a new generation architecture (previous work such as GhostWriter 2015 already applied LSTMs to rap lyric generation), but it makes a notable and clearly differentiated contribution by proposing a comprehensive, multi-faceted evaluation methodology for rap ghostwriting, releasing a 13-artist annotated dataset for style matching, and fully automating a previously semi-automatic rhyme-density similarity metric. These are non-trivial, practical advances over existing work (which focused on generation) and would inform experts concerned with rigorous evaluation of creative-language systems, even though the generation technique itself is not novel.

main_claims: A comprehensive manual evaluation methodology for rap lyric ghostwriting assessing fluency, coherence, and style matching.
An automated evaluation improvement that penalizes repetitive text, fully automating a previously semi-automatic method based on rhyme density and similarity measures.

A publicly released dataset of lyrics for 13 rap artists annotated for stylistic similarity, and an empirical illustration of the methodology on an LSTM-based ghostwriter showing the LSTM generalizes better than an n-gram baseline.
_summary.methodology: Combine manual annotation (line-level fluency and coherence, and page-level style-matching) with an automated evaluation using rhyme density (number of rhymed syllables / total syllables) and maximum similarity regression over training iterations; generate verses from per-artist LSTM models and an n-gram baseline for comparison.
datasets: The Original Hip-Hop (Rap) Lyrics Archive (OHHLA)-derived dataset of lyrics for 13 rap artists
limitations: Automated evaluation cannot assess certain aspects such as vocabulary, tone, and themes preferred by an artist.
Preprocessing was heuristic and the resulting dataset may still contain non-verse text (dialogue or chorus lines).
The LSTM model has difficulty integrating new vocabulary, limiting desirable similarity scores.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - creative language generation (rap lyric ghostwriting)
